comment.body: When time passes or IV falls, the distribution curve gets smaller. OTM options (the majority are OTM) lose deltas. Dealers rebalance their hedges on this (buy back short deltas). Since the world is long (Sell call/buy put), this forces the market higher after events, that raises IV. Of course there are outliers. But generally speaking, after big events, where the investors are well hedged, the market goes higher afterwards. This makes shorting difficult and being long easy.
comment.raw_body: When time passes or IV falls, the distribution curve gets smaller. OTM options (the majority are OTM) lose deltas. Dealers rebalance their hedges on this (buy back short deltas). Since the world is long (Sell call/buy put), this forces the market higher after events, that raises IV. Of course there are outliers. But generally speaking, after big events, where the investors are well hedged, the market goes higher afterwards. This makes shorting difficult and being long easy.

comment.body: For Vix. Vix is a strip of OTM options. Skew influences the calculation (think of OTM puts and their IV). This elevates Vix in comparsion to ATM vol. So you look at ATM vol and Vix will not get there, because of skew in its calculation.
comment.raw_body: For Vix. Vix is a strip of OTM options. Skew influences the calculation (think of OTM puts and their IV). This elevates Vix in comparsion to ATM vol. So you look at ATM vol and Vix will not get there, because of skew in its calculation.

comment.body:
The upmove occurs due to the tightening of the distribution curve as IV falls or time passes, making OTM options less likely to land ITM.
A squeeze, or more specifically a gamma squeeze, can develop when IV becomes so low that investors begin aggressively buying calls. As a result, dealers accumulate increasing short call exposure (negative gamma), forcing them to raise IV as spot moves higher, creating a “spot up, vol up” environment.
As IV rises, gamma decreases, which makes dealer hedging less effective. This breakdown in hedging dynamics can cause a decoupling, or "unpinning" of spot and volatility, accelerating the move and resulting in a true gamma squeeze.
comment.raw_body:
The upmove occurs due to the tightening of the distribution curve as IV falls or time passes, making OTM options less likely to land ITM.
A squeeze, or more specifically a gamma squeeze, can develop when IV becomes so low that investors begin aggressively buying calls. As a result, dealers accumulate increasing short call exposure (negative gamma), forcing them to raise IV as spot moves higher, creating a “spot up, vol up” environment.
As IV rises, gamma decreases, which makes dealer hedging less effective. This breakdown in hedging dynamics can cause a decoupling, or "unpinning" of spot and volatility, accelerating the move and resulting in a true gamma squeeze.

collar has no impact above the weekly ranges. 
if market grinds down, it is vega that goes up. so MMs sell. If vol goes down, delta goes up, buying against the trend = further pressure on vol, also speed effect.
